metadata.title: Measure the Ask Mode/Code Mode Gate: With vs. Without

beats.narration_text: The gate moves cheap work upstream of expensive work. The math is favorable whenever one caught assumption saves one rollback.

The Ask Mode gate has a cost: the time it takes to interrogate a plan. The return: assumptions caught before Code Mode runs are cheaper than rollbacks after it runs. The gate is overhead for trivial one-line fixes. It is not overhead for consequential changes — because consequential changes have wrong assumptions.

claude "Before writing any code: read the existing modules this feature will interact with and propose an implementation plan. State what you will create, what you will modify, what you will not touch, and the two most likely wrong assumptions in your plan. Do not write any code yet." < task_and_codebase.md

props.command: claude "Before writing any code: propose an implementation plan. Name what you will create, modify, not touch, and the two most likely wrong assumptions. Do not write code yet." < task_and_codebase.md

beats.narration_text: gate_comparison.py — runs same task through no-gate path and gate path, records time, correction cycles, rollbacks, final quality score.

props.code:
# compares gate vs no-gate path performance
import gate_runner, timer, quality_scorer

no_gate = gate_runner.run(task, mode='no_gate')
gate = gate_runner.run(task, mode='ask_then_code')

print(f'No-gate: {no_gate.corrections} corrections, {no_gate.rollbacks} rollbacks, {no_gate.minutes}min')
print(f'Gate:    {gate.corrections} corrections, {gate.rollbacks} rollbacks, {gate.minutes}min')

beats.narration_text: No-gate path: 3 correction cycles, 1 rollback, 25 minutes. Gate path: the Ask Mode plan surfaced one wrong assumption — Codex assumed the User model had a username field; it has email. Fixing the assumption in the plan took 2 minutes. Code Mode ran once, no corrections, no rollback. Total: 12 minutes. The gate cost 2 minutes and prevented 13.
Run the gate path on a trivial one-line fix; show the gate is correctly overhead for that case.

One-line fix — update a constant value: gate path takes 3 minutes, no-gate path takes 1 minute. Gate is overhead for trivial changes — no wrong assumptions exist to surface. The calibration: use the gate for consequential changes. Skip it for changes where wrong is immediately visible and rollback is instant.

The gate's value is proportional to the cost of being wrong. Wrong on a one-line constant: 10 seconds to fix. Wrong on a module integration: 20 minutes plus a rollback. The gate is not a ritual — it's an investment whose return depends on what you'd have to roll back without it.
Next: build a three-pass verification checklist that catches what tests miss.
Nik Bear Brown. Brutalist plus Educational AI. nikbearbrown.com.
